name: Dry Lake Wash
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding along a scenic wash in Emery County where agate and jasper specimens are found in streambed gravels and canyon walls. Open to public collecting with 25 lb daily limit.
description:
Dry Lake Wash is a productive rockhounding destination on BLM public land in central Utah's Emery County. The wash cuts through colorful Mesozoic sedimentary formations, exposing excellent agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens in the streambed and eroded canyon walls. This is a free, accessible site requiring no permits, making it ideal for both beginners and experienced rockhounds seeking quality specimens without commercial pressures.

The wash terrain is gently rolling with alternating sandy and rocky sections. Rockhounds work the wash bottom, side gravels, and exposed banks where water erosion has naturally concentrated lighter mineral material. The surrounding landscape features juniper and pinyon pine vegetation typical of Utah's high desert. Most productive collecting happens after seasonal runoff when new material is exposed.

This location offers a true back-country rockhounding experience without the fees or restrictions of commercial dig sites. The colorful banding in agates and jaspers found here reflects the complex geologic history of the region - layers of silica-rich deposits laid down during ancient marine and fluvial environments, later hardened and exposed by uplift and erosion. Specimens range from small pebbles to pieces suitable for cutting and polishing.

Because this is BLM land, collection is personal use only with a 25 lb per day limit. The wash is accessible to vehicles with good clearance, though hiking the wash itself provides the best collecting opportunities. Plan for a full day to explore multiple sections and maximize your finds.
history: Dry Lake Wash lies within the Colorado Plateau, where Jurassic and Cretaceous marine and deltaic deposits were laid down over 100 million years ago. Silica-rich groundwater percolating through these sediments formed the agate and jasper deposits found here today. The wash itself is a product of relatively recent erosion in the past few million years, carving through these consolidated rock layers and concentrating weathered mineral specimens in the streambed.
tips: Work the main wash channel and side gravels where water has naturally sorted and concentrated lighter stones - this is where the best agates and jasper typically accumulate. Look for translucent or banded specimens with red, brown, and tan coloring partially embedded in clay and gravel; specimen quality improves when you dig slightly beneath the surface. Bring a classifier or screen to quickly separate rocks by size and find better pieces faster. The best collecting typically occurs in spring after seasonal water flow has exposed fresh material on the banks.
terrain_notes: Dry Lake Wash flows through relatively gentle terrain at approximately 5,800-6,200 feet elevation with sparse juniper and pinyon pine vegetation. The wash bottom is sandy with scattered rocks, side banks are eroded claystone and siltstone. Walking the wash requires scrambling over scattered boulders and occasional minor obstacles. Parking areas provide access to 0.5-3 miles of collectible wash depending on which sections you explore. Footing is generally stable but requires sturdy hiking boots.
written_directions: From Castle Valley, take Utah State Route 128 south for approximately 8 miles toward the Cisco area. Turn east onto County Road 340 (a well-maintained dirt road) and drive approximately 6 miles. Look for access points and parking pullouts on the right side where the road approaches Dry Lake Wash. The main wash access is marked by a small parking area near mile marker 6 on the county road. High-clearance vehicles are recommended but not required for initial access points.
road_conditions: County road to access is well-maintained dirt road, suitable for passenger vehicles in dry conditions. Some washboard texture present. High-clearance recommended for exploring upper tributaries.
parking_notes: Small dirt pullouts available near the main wash access points along County Road 340. Parking capacity approximately 4-6 vehicles at primary access. No developed parking facilities or amenities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment use. Collect only loose stones and surface specimens; do not break outcrop. Pack out all trash. Respect private property boundaries if present.
rules:
Personal use collecting only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or heavy equipment
Collect only loose material - do not break outcrop
Pack out all trash
Respect all boundary markers
nearest_services: Castle Valley (8 miles north) has limited services. Moab (35 miles northwest) has full services including gas, food, lodging, and supply shops.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with easy access to the main wash. Parking areas are at ground level. Walking the wash involves moderate scrambling over scattered boulders and minor elevation changes. No wheelchair access beyond parking area. Sturdy hiking boots recommended. Best for those comfortable with uneven, rocky terrain. The site is very accessible for most age groups and fitness levels.

faq.question: What types of gemstones can you find at Dry Lake Wash in Utah?
faq.answer: Dry Lake Wash is known for producing quality agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. The agates typically display banding in red, brown, and tan colors with varying degrees of translucency. Jasper pieces range from opaque to semi-translucent and are highly valued for cutting and polishing. Loose quartz specimens are also common throughout the wash.

faq.question: How much does it cost to rockhound at Dry Lake Wash, and do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Dry Lake Wash is completely free with no permit required. The site is on BLM public land where personal use collecting is allowed under FLPMA regulations. There are no fees, passes, or advance permits needed - simply arrive and start collecting within the 25 lb daily limit.

faq.question: What is the best season for rockhounding at Dry Lake Wash near Castle Valley?
faq.answer: October through April is the optimal season. During these cooler months, you avoid extreme desert heat and take advantage of spring runoff (March-April) when water erosion exposes fresh material along the wash banks. Summer heat (May-September) is intense in this high desert location and makes collecting uncomfortable.

faq.question: Can you bring your family and dogs to Dry Lake Wash for rockhounding?
faq.answer: Yes, Dry Lake Wash is very family-friendly and dog-friendly. It's an easy walk suitable for children ages 6 and up, and there are no restrictions on bringing dogs. The loose specimens and open terrain make this an ideal introduction to rockhounding for families. Just ensure you stay hydrated and bring sun protection.

faq.question: What rockhounding tools and supplies should you bring to Dry Lake Wash?
faq.answer: Bring a small classifier or screen to sift gravels efficiently and find better specimens faster. A digging tool or small shovel helps expose material slightly beneath the surface. Wear sturdy hiking boots for the rocky terrain. Pack plenty of water (at least 2-3 liters), sun protection, and a backpack for carrying your 25 lb daily limit. A field bag and newspaper help organize specimens.

faq.question: Is Dry Lake Wash a good rockhounding spot for beginners with no experience?
faq.answer: Absolutely. Dry Lake Wash is one of Utah's best beginner sites because specimens are accessible on the surface and in streambed gravels without special skills or equipment. The free, no-permit access and easy terrain make it stress-free for learning to identify agates and jaspers. Most beginners find quality specimens on their first visit and can hike the wash comfortably in 2-3 hours.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding for agate, jasper, and chalcedony near Castle Valley, Utah. No permit required. Perfect for beginners and families.
